title: File Access Permissions Quiz
description: Test your knowledge about the critical role of file access permissions and secure access control in modern web applications.

questions.question: Which of these is the primary way to manage file access permissions for a single user in a web application?
options: Providing everyone with read permissions for all files.
By storing a 'userId' on the file record in your database and checking it against the current authenticated user on every request.
By automatically generating a separate folder for each user.
It's not possible to have separate permissions for each user.

questions.question: What's the best way to prevent 'Insecure Direct Object Reference' (IDOR) attacks when serving files?
options: By automatically renaming all files to a random string.
By using sequential IDs for every file URL (e.g., /files/1, /files/2).
By using UUIDs or other long, random strings for file IDs in URLs and always verifying permissions in the backend.
It's not a real security problem.

questions.question: Which permission is needed for a user to actually open and see the content of a file on a UNIX file system?
options: Read (r)
Write (w)
Execute (x)
All of the above.

questions.question: In Cloud Storage like Amazon S3, what is used to define exactly who can perform actions on specific objects/buckets?
options: HTTP headers
IAM Policies and Bucket Policies
CSS variables
A type of database indexing.

questions.question: What is the 'Deny by Default' principle in the context of file permissions?
options: It means all files are accessible to everyone by default.
It means access to a file is blocked unless a user is explicitly granted permission or the file is explicitly marked as public.
It means users are only allowed to upload files, not download them.
None of the above.
